name: B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan builds logically from foundations (CS 100, IS 117, IS 265) through core IS competencies (IS 218 web apps, IS 331 databases, IS 390 requirements analysis) to advanced/management courses (IS 455 IS Management & Business Processes, IS 465 Advanced Information Systems) and a senior capstone (IS 491/IT 491). Business fundamentals (ACCT, ECON, FIN, MGMT, MRKT, HRM) interleave sensibly with the IS track, and a deepest prerequisite chain of 7 confirms genuine vertical progression. The blend is well-ordered with no abrupt jumps; the only softness is a few unlabeled general-elective and GER slots.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate the general-elective and GER placeholder slots with recommended course options so students see how those choices reinforce the business-plus-IS progression rather than appearing as generic filler.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is real and well-formed, with per-course depths spanning 0 to 7 and a deepestPrereqChain of 7 that reflects a coherent path into advanced IS (IS 465 at depth 7). However, metrics.missingCourseCount is 4, with missingCoursesSample listing GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, and YWCC 307 as dangling references not resolvable in the undergraduate catalog. GER 200/GER 300 are general-education placeholders rather than true course codes, while YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are one-credit seminars that appear in the plan but do not resolve; none break the core IS chain, but they are genuine dangling references, and a chain depth of 7 is on the deep side and worth auditing for over-constraint.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references by mapping GER 200 and GER 300 to their canonical general-education requirement pages and confirming YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are cataloged, then verify the depth-7 chain into IS 465 does not over-constrain scheduling.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no learning-outcomes, objectives, or overview field of any kind; only the structured plan of study and course list are present. For a catalog-quality page, the absence of explicit program-level learning outcomes is a real deficiency because prospective students and assessors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program-level learning outcomes (for example, four to six statements covering IS analysis and design, database and web application development, business process management, and data-driven decision making) directly on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: metrics.creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 and summedTermCredits (120.0) equals statedTotalCredits (120.0), so there is no internal inconsistency. Term loads are well balanced, ranging from 12 to 16 credits with most terms at 15-16, and the mix spans general education (ENGL, MATH, science GER, HSS), a business core (ACCT/ECON/FIN/MGMT/MRKT/HRM), an IS core, and general electives. The one point held back reflects that the gen-ed versus major versus elective totals are not summarized explicitly for the reader.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-distribution summary (e.g., general education X, business core Y, IS core Z, electives W) so the transparent 120-credit total is visible at a glance rather than only inferable from the term-by-term plan.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan is organized into clearly labeled terms (First Year 1st Semester through Fourth Year 2nd Semester) with per-term credit totals, which aids readability. However, several parsing artifacts degrade the page: GER entries are split across multiple rows with raw catalog URL fragments (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER (http://...' followed by 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)'), alternative options render as bare 'or ACCT 115 or...' lines, and many rows carry null codes. There is also no program description or narrative overview.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parsing so GER requirements render as single labeled rows without embedded URLs, collapse the 'or' alternatives into clear either/or pairs, and add a brief program overview paragraph at the top of the page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current information-systems and business practice: IT 310 E-Commerce Technology, MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics, IS 247 and IS 375 user-experience and user-needs courses, IS 218 Building Web Applications, IS 331 database design, IS 390 requirements analysis, IS 455 IS management and business processes, and IT 120 networking, all grounded in a solid business core (marketing, corporate finance, organizational behavior, international business). This is a modern, employable blend. Notable gaps are the absence of dedicated cybersecurity, cloud, or explicit data-science/AI coursework beyond introductory analytics.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or list an elective option in cybersecurity, cloud computing, or applied machine learning/data science to close the most visible modern gap and keep the analytics thread current.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every one of the 33 sampled courses (ACCT 115 through MRKT 330) has a null description, so no course on the page provides any explanatory text about content, topics, or scope. This is a uniform and serious presentation gap: titles alone must carry all meaning, and several are cryptic abbreviations (e.g., 'Principles Of Fin Accountng', 'IS Mgmt & Business Processes').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite relationships exist and were parsed into a depth graph (course depths range 0 to 7), but no course exposes explicit, human-readable prerequisite text, and metrics.missingCourseCount is 4 with GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, and YWCC 307 dangling. The combination of unstated prerequisite text and dangling references means students cannot reliably see or verify what is required before each course.

topSuggestions: Publish course descriptions for all courses; every one of the 33 sampled courses currently has no description, which is the single largest gap on the page.
Add explicit program-level learning outcomes/objectives, which are entirely absent from the program data.
Resolve the four dangling course references (GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) so prerequisite and requirement links all point to cataloged entries.
Fix plan-of-study parsing artifacts so GER requirements render as single clean rows without embedded URLs and 'or' alternatives display as clear either/or choices.
Add a program overview and a credit-distribution summary (gen-ed vs. business core vs. IS core vs. electives) to frame the transparent, reconciled 120-credit plan.
